concise:
Ayah 1–13 · linked video: 01. An-Nur (Ayah 1-13)

Surah An-Nur: Understanding Divine Laws and Social Protection

Ayah 1

سُورَةٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهَا وَفَرَضْنَـٰهَا وَأَنزَلْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

This surah marks a significant transition in the Quran’s revelation. After a lengthy sequence of Makkan surahs—from Surah Yunus through Surah Al-Mu’minun—Surah An-Nur arrives as the first predominantly Madinan revelation. While there is scholarly discussion about certain ayahs in Surah Al-Hajj, the content from Surah 10 through Surah 23 has been overwhelmingly Makkan. Now, approximately 18-19 years into the prophetic mission, we encounter this pivotal surah.

The timing is crucial. Most scholars place this revelation in the sixth year after the Hijrah, after the Battle of the Trench (Al-Ahzab) but before the Treaty of Hudaybiyah. Some suggest it could be as early as the fifth year, coinciding with the Battle of Al-Ahzab itself. What makes this surah particularly significant is its relationship to Surah Al-Ahzab (33), which comes later in the Quranic arrangement but deals with complementary social legislation. These two surahs function as sister revelations, each completing what the other begins regarding the social fabric of the Muslim community.

Allah ﷻ begins with an extraordinary introduction: “A surah We have sent down.” The phrasing itself is remarkable—as though the community had been waiting for this specific revelation, as though they knew it couldn’t simply be scattered ayahs within another surah but required its own dedicated space. This anticipation will become clearer as we examine the historical context.

The phrase وَفَرَضْنَـٰهَا (and We have mandated it) carries particular weight. The root ف-ر-ض relates to carving or marking wood, creating something permanent and unchangeable. When something is set in stone—or carved in wood—it cannot be altered. Allah ﷻ is establishing that these are not mere suggestions but divine mandates, obligations that carry the full weight of His authority.

Notice that when Allah ﷻ gives commands elsewhere in the Quran, He typically states them directly without preamble. The command itself implies obligation. But here, before revealing any specific law, He declares: “This is a magnificent surah. We have sent it down. We have made it mandatory.” This layered emphasis serves a purpose—it prepares the believers to receive difficult legislation with the seriousness it demands.

وَأَنزَلْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (And We have sent down in it clear signs) addresses a potential objection before it arises. These ayahs will contain بَيِّنَـٰت—signs that are clear and unambiguous in themselves. There will be no room for multiple interpretations or attempts to find loopholes. The guidance is explicit.

لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (so that you may be mindful) concludes the introduction with purpose. The laws about to be revealed require the Muslim community—now maturing in Madinah—to take serious note, to remember, to internalize. This is not information to be passively received but actively engaged with and implemented.

No other surah addressing the Muslim community has been given this level of preliminary emphasis. Among all the social legislation in the Quran, none begins with such a powerful declaration of importance. Yet how many Muslims today have even heard of Surah An-Nur? Those slightly more knowledgeable might recognize the Ayat An-Nur (the Verse of Light), but remain unfamiliar with the rest—unaware of what else surrounds that heart in its ribcage.

Ayah 2

ٱلزَّانِيَةُ وَٱلزَّانِى فَٱجْلِدُوا۟ كُلَّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا مِا۟ئَةَ جَلْدَةٍ ۖ وَلَا تَأْخُذْكُم بِهِمَا رَأْفَةٌ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذَابَهُمَا طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

The surah now addresses its first major subject: laws protecting the sanctity of marriage and social decency. Understanding the progression of revelation is essential here. We are now 18-19 years into the prophetic mission—12-13 years in Makkah and 5-6 years in Madinah—yet the explicit legal punishments for zina (fornication and adultery) are only now being revealed.

This timing teaches us something fundamental about Islamic legislation: the decency of a society cannot be implemented purely through law. For well over a decade, the Makkan society received constant spiritual reminders about modesty, shame, and the consequences of indecency. The issue of حَيَاء (modesty) and fighting shamelessness has been a core subject from the very beginning of revelation. The laws regarding hijab and these punishments are not the foundation—they are the completion, the legal framework that finalizes a subject already deeply embedded in the community’s consciousness.

Consider the wisdom in this progression. When you impose laws on people who don’t understand their purpose, who haven’t internalized the value behind them, who remain unaware of the spiritual consequences, you create resistance rather than compliance. The 1920s Prohibition era in America demonstrates this perfectly—when alcohol was banned without adequate social preparation, its production actually increased. People found ways around the law because they didn’t value the principle behind it.

But when the Quran’s prohibition of alcohol was finally revealed to the Muslims, despite living in a desert environment where liquids were precious commodities, despite wine representing years of agricultural investment, the streets of Madinah flowed with discarded alcohol. People even gagged themselves, desperate to remove from their stomachs what they had consumed before the revelation came. Aisha (RA) remarked about how the women of the Muhajirun immediately tore their aprons to fashion head coverings the moment the ayah of hijab was revealed.

This is the difference between law imposed on an unprepared society and law received by a spiritually grounded community. The Makkan society wasn’t pure—it had the munafiqun (hypocrites) within it—but it had been educated in the foundations of faith for over a decade. They understood the afterlife consequences, which are far more important than any worldly punishment.

ٱلزَّانِيَةُ وَٱلزَّانِى (The female fornicator and the male fornicator)—the ayah addresses both genders equally. The term زِنَا refers to illegitimate sexual relations between unmarried individuals. For married individuals who commit adultery, the punishment came through the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ: stoning to death, a continuation of Mosaic law that the Quran confirmed by not amending it.

فَٱجْلِدُوا۟ كُلَّ وَٰحِدٍ مِّنْهُمَا مِا۟ئَةَ جَلْدَةٍ (Then flog each one of them with one hundred lashes). The word جَلْد relates to skin—the punishment affects the surface, not causing deep wounds or permanent physical damage. It’s a public humiliation and painful deterrent, but not maiming or execution.

وَلَا تَأْخُذْكُم بِهِمَا رَأْفَةٌ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ (And let not compassion for them take hold of you in the matter of Allah’s religion). This is a striking command. Natural human sympathy might make the executor hesitate, might make them deliver lighter blows or stop before completing the punishment. Allah ﷻ addresses this directly: when it comes time to execute the judgment of Allah ﷻ, compassion cannot interfere with justice.

Consider the historical context: Aisha (RA) had been accused of impropriety, and she was waiting—still a young woman—for Allah ﷻ to reveal her innocence. Her father, Abu Bakr (RA), was waiting. The Prophet ﷺ himself could not make a judgment without divine revelation. And when the revelation finally came, it began not with her vindication, but with harsh punishments. The first thing the hypocrites heard was “one hundred lashes”—words that must have pleased them.

But the structure is deliberate. Before comfort comes discipline. Before vindication comes the establishment of divine law.

إِن كُنتُمْ تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ (If you truly believe in Allah and the Last Day). The execution of this difficult punishment is now tied to iman itself. If your faith is genuine, you will not let misplaced mercy prevent you from upholding Allah’s law.

وَلْيَشْهَدْ عَذَابَهُمَا طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (And let a group of believers witness their punishment). This is not to be done secretly. A significant group must witness the punishment so that it serves as a deterrent for society. The public nature of the punishment sends a message: this is not a small matter.

The modern question arises: “What’s the big deal? So someone has a boyfriend or girlfriend—why is this so serious?” Allah ﷻ answers by making the punishment public and severe. The spiritual consequences have already been established through years of revelation about the evils of shamelessness. But for those with weak faith or hypocrisy in their hearts—those who don’t value afterlife consequences—there must be a worldly deterrent so severe that they understand: if this is the punishment in this world, imagine what awaits in the next.

Some scholars note that this worldly punishment actually serves as a mercy—it allows people to pay for their sin in this life so they may be absolved in the next.

However, this law cannot be executed without proper understanding of fiqh (Islamic jurisprudence) and the complete framework of Islamic teachings. We hear news reports of women being stoned or flogged in certain countries, with people declaring “This is Islamic law!” But our religion is not one isolated law—it’s an entire integrated system. You cannot extract one piece and implement it while ignoring everything else. That is hypocrisy.

Islam has established social safeguards for women from the very beginning. When someone engages in prostitution out of desperate poverty, when their baby is starving and they have no other option, who should be punished? Not the woman—the society that failed to implement the Islamic teachings about caring for orphans, protecting the vulnerable, and ensuring no one is driven to desperation. If you want to implement Shariah, implement all of it—the social support systems, the financial protections, the community obligations. You cannot pick one harsh punishment and ignore all the mercy that surrounds it.

Ayah 3

ٱلزَّانِى لَا يَنكِحُ إِلَّا زَانِيَةً أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً وَٱلزَّانِيَةُ لَا يَنكِحُهَآ إِلَّا زَانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ ۚ وَحُرِّمَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah addresses the natural social consequences of such behavior. ٱلزَّانِى لَا يَنكِحُ إِلَّا زَانِيَةً (The fornicator will not marry except a fornicator) or أَوْ مُشْرِكَةً (or a polytheist woman). Similarly, ٱلزَّانِيَةُ لَا يَنكِحُهَآ إِلَّا زَانٍ أَوْ مُشْرِكٌ (the fornicator woman will not be married except by a fornicator or a polytheist).

This is not a command creating a new legal requirement—it’s not saying “if you’ve committed zina, you must now find another person who has committed zina and marry them.” Rather, it’s a statement of natural social reality: people of similar character gravitate toward one another. The filthy seek the filthy; those without regard for Allah’s boundaries in marriage will naturally pair with others who share that disregard.

The deeper meaning emerges when we consider marriage itself. Allah ﷻ described our mother Hawwa (AS) not by her name but by her relationship: the wife of Adam (AS). Marriage is the first law, one of the foundational institutions of human society. When Allah ﷻ speaks of the marriage contract, He calls it مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا—a strong, weighty covenant. This same term appears when Allah ﷻ describes the covenant He took from the prophets to deliver His message. The marriage contract is that serious.

So when someone violates the sanctity of marriage through zina, they demonstrate that they don’t value this weighty covenant. If they can violate one mithaq, what stops them from violating another? If marriage doesn’t matter to them, why would it matter whether they marry a Muslim woman or not? The person who treats marriage casually has already shown their character.

وَحُرِّمَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (And such behavior is forbidden to the believers). The believers must have discrimination in whom they marry and how they conduct themselves. They must value the sanctity of the institution.

An important note: when Allah ﷻ calls someone الزَّانِى or الزَّانِيَةُ, we cannot simply apply these labels to anyone we suspect. These are legal terms for application of divine law, and we cannot know who truly deserves these labels until there is absolute proof according to the standards about to be revealed.

Ayah 4

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ فَٱجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمَـٰنِينَ جَلْدَةً وَلَا تَقْبَلُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَهَـٰدَةً أَبَدًا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

Now comes the protection against false accusations. وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ (And those who throw accusations against chaste women). The verb يَرْمُونَ is powerful—it means to throw or hurl, the same word used for shooting arrows or throwing spears. It’s an act of war. This is not casual speech; it’s an attack.

ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ comes from حِصْن—a fort or fortress. A woman who is محصنة is one protected within the fortress of her family and her dignity. She is sheltered, guarded, respectable. To accuse such a woman is to launch an assault on her protection, on her honor, on her entire household.

ثُمَّ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ (Then they do not produce four witnesses)—here is the condition that makes false accusation nearly impossible to get away with and true cases nearly impossible to prove. Four witnesses must have seen the actual act. How likely is that? It’s virtually impossible. And Allah ﷻ made it that way deliberately.

Why? Because these matters should not become public spectacle. Even if such acts occur, Allah ﷻ will deal with them on the Day of Judgment. Only in extremely rare circumstances—where four reliable witnesses actually saw the act—should these private sins become public knowledge requiring legal action.

فَٱجْلِدُوهُمْ ثَمَـٰنِينَ جَلْدَةً (Then flog them with eighty lashes). The person who makes an accusation without producing four witnesses receives eighty lashes themselves. Someone who simply says “I think those two…” or “I heard that she and he…” should receive eighty lashes. The punishment for the accusation is severe because the damage caused by such accusations is severe.

Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) was once in the marketplace when someone came to him and said, “You know what’s happening behind this curtain? A man and a woman…” Umar (RA) struck him and said, “Why are you exposing it? That’s not what you’re supposed to do!” We don’t broadcast the shameful things happening in society as though they’re entertainment.

This is a nuanced and difficult principle to explain. We don’t hide problems—we must acknowledge them and address them. We need counselors, educators, and helpers ready to assist those struggling with these issues. But there’s a difference between acknowledging a problem exists and spreading specific details that trivialize the sin.

Sometimes speakers say, “You know what our youth are doing? They’re doing this and that…” spelling out explicit details. People sitting in the congregation think, “I didn’t need to know that.” What’s the purpose? To shock people? When such information is spread publicly, several harmful things happen: it trivializes the sin, it exposes people to ideas they might not have considered, and it destroys innocence prematurely.

Children who are eight years old hear these explicit descriptions and become curious. When curiosity develops too early, natural shame doesn’t get the chance to grow properly. This is why the cartoon industry that pushes dating concepts on six-year-olds through Nickelodeon and Disney is so destructive—“she has a crush on him, he likes her, they’re going to prom together”—making romance trivial from childhood.

And we can do the same damage not from Disney but from the minbar. We can talk about serious sins as though they’re casual topics of conversation: “Boys and girls are doing this and that, this is the new trend…” No. The imam, the counselor should be aware, should point people to proper resources, and should speak about such matters with dignity.

Notice that the Quran uses approximately ten or eleven terms for sexual intercourse, but never once does it use explicit language. Everything is alluded to, nothing is spelled out. There’s a philosophy today that says, “If you don’t say explicit words, you create mystery and curiosity around them, so you should use explicit language to remove its power.” This is false. These are filthy words, and filth should not be normalized. Its power comes not from mystery but from its repulsiveness—I don’t go near it because it stinks.

Allah ﷻ says: unless they produce four witnesses, don’t open your mouth. Don’t say, “I heard she and that guy…” or “I heard there’s a lot of dating at that school…” Don’t say it. Keep silent. If it were subject to Allah’s law in this world, the cleansing of that sin would require what? Eighty lashes. It’s not “just saying something”—it’s an assault.

وَلَا تَقْبَلُوا۟ لَهُمْ شَهَـٰدَةً أَبَدًا (And never accept their testimony again). These people become unreliable witnesses in the court of Islamic law forever. Whether it’s testimony about a murder, a financial dispute, or any other matter, they are disqualified permanently.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (And those are the inherently corrupt ones). They’re not just mistaken or misguided—they are فَـٰسِقُونَ, people who think it’s no big deal to casually discuss others’ sexual impropriety. This is deep corruption of character.

Ayah 5

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

But Allah ﷻ never closes the door on redemption. إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ (Except those who repent after that) وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ (and reform themselves). True tawbah isn’t just words—it’s demonstrated through corrected behavior. When you can see that they have genuinely changed, that their character has been reformed, they can be accepted back into society as reliable members of the community.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful). After establishing harsh punishments and serious consequences, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His nature. He is غَفُورٌ—repeatedly, continuously forgiving. He is رَّحِيمٌ—always merciful. The door to His forgiveness remains open even after the most serious transgressions.

Ayahs 6-9

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ شُهَدَآءُ إِلَّآ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَشَهَـٰدَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةُ أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

وَيَدْرَؤُا۟ عَنْهَا ٱلْعَذَابَ أَن تَشْهَدَ أَرْبَعَ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةَ أَنَّ غَضَبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

Now comes a unique provision for a specific circumstance. وَٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ أَزْوَٰجَهُمْ (And those who accuse their own spouses). A husband accuses his wife. The natural objection arises: “How am I supposed to have four witnesses? This happened in the privacy of our home. How could anyone else have seen it?”

Allah ﷻ provides a special procedure. وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ شُهَدَآءُ إِلَّآ أَنفُسُهُمْ (And they have no witnesses except themselves), then فَشَهَـٰدَةُ أَحَدِهِمْ أَرْبَعُ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ (the testimony of one of them shall be to swear by Allah four times) إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (that he is truthful).

The husband’s testimony counts as four, but each time he testifies, he must invoke Allah ﷻ explicitly: “I swear by Allah I saw it. I swear by Allah I saw it.” Four times. Remember the principle Allah ﷻ established elsewhere: لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ (Allah will not hold you accountable for unintentional oaths) وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ (but He will hold you accountable for the oaths you solemnly make). When you stand in public court and swear by Allah ﷻ, He will hold you to account for that oath.

وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةُ أَنَّ لَعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ (And the fifth testimony that the curse of Allah be upon him) إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (if he is lying). The fifth statement is different from the previous four. He must declare publicly: “May Allah’s curse fall upon me if I am lying.” He invokes divine curse upon himself.

Now the wife’s turn. وَيَدْرَؤُا۟ عَنْهَا ٱلْعَذَابَ (And it will avert punishment from her) أَن تَشْهَدَ أَرْبَعَ شَهَـٰدَٰتٍۭ بِٱللَّهِ (that she testifies four times by Allah) إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (that he is lying). She swears four times: “By Allah, he is lying.”

And her fifth: وَٱلْخَـٰمِسَةَ أَنَّ غَضَبَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَآ (And the fifth that the wrath of Allah be upon her) إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (if he is truthful). She invokes Allah’s anger upon herself if her husband is telling the truth.

This is a spiritual remedy for a social problem. You might think Islam only offers physical punishment, but here we see two types of consequences: worldly and in the afterlife. In cases like these, Allah ﷻ relegates the matter to the Hereafter.

The husband swears four times plus invokes curse. The wife swears four times plus invokes wrath. The case is neutralized—no worldly punishment can be executed because she has already guaranteed herself divine punishment if she’s lying. The law says: leave her alone in this world. If she’s lying, she hasn’t gotten away with anything. And if she’s telling the truth and he was wrong, he’s condemned himself to divine curse forever.

Who will have confidence in laws like these where punishment comes in the Akhirah? Only those who have true iman. When she says “May the wrath of Allah be upon me” and walks away apparently free, only someone with faith understands: she didn’t get away with anything. She’s condemned herself. The same with the husband—if he invoked curse falsely, he’s guaranteed himself Hellfire. Whatever pain he caused her is nothing compared to what he’s inflicted upon himself.

This is how Islam combines spiritual and physical law. This is what Shariah truly does—it doesn’t separate the seen from the unseen, the worldly from the eternal.

Ayah 10

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ

This ayah uses an incomplete conditional structure—a powerful rhetorical device. وَلَوْلَا (And had it not been for) فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ (Allah’s favor upon you) وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ (and His mercy)…

The sentence doesn’t complete. “Had it not been for Allah’s favor and mercy…” then what? The consequence is left unsaid because it’s unspeakable. The calamity, the catastrophe you would have faced had these laws not been revealed—the chaos in your community, the destruction of families, the spreading of accusations—all of this is too terrible to even articulate.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ حَكِيمٌ (And indeed Allah is the Accepter of repentance, the All-Wise). After describing these difficult situations and their remedies, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His nature: He continuously accepts repentance, and His wisdom is perfect. Every law He has given, every procedure He has established, comes from perfect wisdom.

Ayah 11

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو بِٱلْإِفْكِ عُصْبَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ۚ لَا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَّكُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُم مَّا ٱكْتَسَبَ مِنَ ٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِى تَوَلَّىٰ كِبْرَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ لَهُۥ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

Now we arrive at the vindication of Aisha (RA), though to fully appreciate these ayahs, we must understand what happened. The Prophet ﷺ was returning from a military campaign—most accounts suggest it was the expedition to Banu Al-Mustaliq. Aisha (RA) traveled with him in a covered howdah (a carriage-like structure) mounted on a camel, providing her privacy during travel.

During a rest stop, she left to relieve herself and realized she had dropped her necklace—the one the Prophet ﷺ had given her, originally from her mother Khadijah (RA). It had immense sentimental value. She went searching for it in the darkness. When she returned, the caravan had departed. The men who loaded her howdah onto the camel hadn’t realized she wasn’t inside—she was light in weight, still quite young.

She did the intelligent thing: she stayed at the rest stop, knowing that if she wandered, they would never find her. She waited there through the night. The next morning, Safwan ibn Al-Muattal (RA), the scout who traveled behind the caravan to ensure nothing was left behind, discovered her. He recognized her (the ayahs of hijab mandating face-covering for the Mothers of the Believers hadn’t yet been revealed, though she was already practicing modesty). He said nothing, simply brought his camel down, turned away while she mounted, and they traveled to catch up with the caravan.

Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, the chief of the hypocrites—a man who had been humiliated multiple times in Madinah due to his corrupt character—saw a golden opportunity. He didn’t make explicit accusations; he simply went around saying things like “She didn’t come back from this trip clean…” and “There’s no way nothing happened between them…” Whispered to one person here, another there.

At first, people rejected it: “No, no, that’s impossible.” But he persisted: “Well… I don’t know… it’s possible, isn’t it?” And the conversation began spreading. It was juicy controversy—who cheated on whom, scandal, gossip. This is why tabloids like the National Enquirer make money when serious newspapers struggle. Shaytan spreads such accusations, and they become wildfire.

The slander became an earthquake in Madinah. Understand what was at stake: the dignity of Aisha (RA) is the dignity of the Prophet ﷺ, and his dignity is the dignity of Islam itself. Her character being questioned meant Islam itself was being questioned—there was no separation. For any husband to hear such accusations about his wife is torture. The Prophet ﷺ compared the pain of a wife’s infidelity to the punishment of Hellfire—that level of torture.

Aisha (RA)—one of the most innocent, pure women who ever lived, so sheltered that such thoughts wouldn’t even cross her mind, raised in Abu Bakr’s (RA) household and then in the Prophet’s ﷺ home—was crushed when she learned of the accusations. The slander spread so widely that when the Prophet ﷺ asked her to respond, she said: “I can’t say anything. If I tell the truth and say I’m innocent, you’ll think I’m lying. If I say I’m guilty, you’ll believe me. So whether I deny it or admit it, I lose. All I can say is what Ya’qub (AS) said: فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ (Patience is beautiful). The only beauty left for me is patience, because I know patience has beautiful reward, and only Allah can help me now.”

She separated herself, living with her mother, not even staying with the Prophet ﷺ during this time. Then finally, the ayahs came down. The Prophet ﷺ was overjoyed. Her mother came to tell her the news. And Aisha (RA) said something remarkable: “I don’t have to thank him. He didn’t make the ayahs come down. He has no choice in the matter. I have to thank Allah.”

So clear was her understanding of where revelation comes from.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو بِٱلْإِفْكِ (Indeed, those who came with the slander). الْإِفْك comes from إِفْك—to turn from the right direction. You’re heading toward your destination, you can see it clearly ahead, then suddenly you take a random turn: “Maybe I should explore this way.” It’s deviation from known truth. Those who came with this blatant lie, this fabricated slander—

عُصْبَةٌ مِّنكُمْ (are a band from among you). عُصْبَة refers to a gang, a group with collective strength. We usually think of gangs in terms of physical intimidation—tough people with muscles. But there’s another kind of strength: social power, the ability to spread rumors, to intimidate through gossip. People don’t want to mess with them because “they’ll spread rumors about me.” This is also عُصْبَة, a gang with social weapons.

لَا تَحْسَبُوهُ شَرًّا لَّكُم (Do not consider it bad for you). Don’t assume this horrible tragedy that’s occurred is ultimately harmful to you.

بَلْ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (Rather, it is good for you). How? Allah ﷻ allowed this situation to occur so that these timeless ayahs would be revealed. Without this specific circumstance, this legislation would never have come down. Your pain becomes guidance for generations. Your suffering becomes their protection. Your difficulty becomes their joy.

Every time a Muslim learns these ayahs, every time they’re taught, every time they’re implemented—who is rewarded? Those who suffered in this situation. Aisha’s (RA) account is filled with rewards every time these ayahs are recited, studied, or applied. She was part of the lesson plan. Abu Bakr (RA) is rewarded. The Prophet ﷺ is rewarded. They had to be in this difficult situation for us to learn these eternal lessons.

لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُم مَّا ٱكْتَسَبَ مِنَ ٱلْإِثْمِ (Each person among them will have what they earned of the sin). Every individual who participated in spreading the slander—even some noble Companions got caught up in it; Hassan ibn Thabit (RA), the poet of the Prophet ﷺ, said something and was influenced by it—each will be held accountable for their share.

وَٱلَّذِى تَوَلَّىٰ كِبْرَهُۥ مِنْهُمْ (And the one who took upon himself the greater part of it) لَهُۥ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (will have a tremendous punishment). The one who carried the lion’s share—Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief orchestrator—for him awaits enormous, tremendous punishment.

Ayah 12

لَّوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ ظَنَّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ خَيْرًا وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ مُّبِينٌ

Here Allah ﷻ demonstrates one of the miraculous rhetorical features of the Quran. لَّوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ (Why is it that when you heard it)—second person, addressing the community directly.

Then the ayah shifts: ظَنَّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ خَيْرًا (the believing men and believing women should have thought good of themselves)—third person.

Why the shift? Because Allah ﷻ is saying: “Why didn’t you—when you heard it—think good?” But then by switching to third person, He indicates: “If you didn’t think good, then your iman wasn’t strong enough. This is what the true believers would have done—not you people. You disappointed Me.”

It’s as though Allah ﷻ is saying: “Real believers would have immediately thought good. But you? You just heard it and had no reaction.”

بِأَنفُسِهِمْ خَيْرًا (Good of themselves/their own people). The believing men and women should have thought well of their own community members, should have assumed the best.

وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِفْكٌ مُّبِينٌ (And said, ‘This is clearly a lie, an obvious slander.’“). If true believers had been present, they would have said:”This is ridiculous. That’s our Mother. That’s a decent Muslim woman. That’s the dignity of Islam. Why are you even talking like this? Open your mouth again and you’ll hear from me."

This controversy, aside from the specific historical incident, now becomes a matter of faith for all Muslims. When people attack the dignity of other Muslims, it becomes a matter of our iman to say no, to refuse participation in such conversations.

Imagine: you’re sitting at an iftar gathering, waiting for the food to arrive. To pass time, you either play on your phone or engage in conversation. And what’s the conversation? “Did you hear about so-and-so? And that sister? And this family?” The conversation flows like entertainment—like watching television, one insult after another. You might not even participate actively, just sit back and listen to the show.

No. Open your mouth and say: “We shouldn’t do this. We should have benefit of the doubt for people. Come on, are you wasting all your fasting? Where’s your iman?” Be brave enough to say it. People might respond: “We’re just talking, it’s not a big deal.” But it is a big deal.

When the believers start realizing this was a serious matter of iman, they begin to understand the weight of what they allowed to happen.

Ayah 13

لَّوْلَا جَآءُو عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشُّهَدَآءِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ

لَّوْلَا جَآءُو عَلَيْهِ بِأَرْبَعَةِ شُهَدَآءَ (Why did they not bring four witnesses to it?). You might think the requirement for four witnesses was only just revealed in this surah. But actually, it had been established years earlier. In Surah An-Nisa (4:15), revealed in the early Madinan period, Allah ﷻ already commanded: وَٱلَّـٰتِى يَأْتِينَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مِن نِّسَآئِكُمْ فَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنكُمْ (And those who commit immorality among your women—bring against them four witnesses from among you).

That was two or three years before this incident. The requirement was already known. But the people didn’t care about that earlier revelation because it didn’t include the punishments we’re now learning. So Allah ﷻ had to reveal the complete penal code—you won’t learn except through tough love? Then here’s the tough revelation.

This teaches us something essential about the Quran’s progression. Allah ﷻ is the ultimate Teacher, and like any wise teacher, He observes His students. A teacher might know on the first day which student will be trouble, that this will be a difficult year. But he starts with positive reinforcement, gentle reminders, hoping the student will correct himself.

The teacher knows—from experience with similar students before—that gentle reminders probably won’t work. This student is predisposed to need firmer discipline. But the teacher gives him the courtesy of not being harsh immediately. Later, when it becomes clear that soft words aren’t enough, the teacher says: “You’re going to be expelled.” Only then does the student become serious: “No, no, I’ll do my homework, I’ll show up, I’ll behave.”

This is the progression of divine teaching. Allah ﷻ knows human beings better than they know themselves. But He gives them the courtesy of not revealing the full extent of the law immediately. First: bring four witnesses. When people ignore that, when the situation demands it, then: bring four witnesses or face eighty lashes yourselves.

فَإِذْ لَمْ يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشُّهَدَآءِ (So since they did not bring the witnesses) فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (then those, in Allah’s sight, are the liars).

Whether they think they’re telling the truth, whether their friends believe them, whether they sound convincing—none of that matters anymore. Allah ﷻ says: they are liars. He is the final Judge. A lawyer might think his client is truthful, the jury might be convinced, the judge might deliberate—but when Allah ﷻ declares someone a liar, the case is closed.

As far as the law of Allah ﷻ is concerned, these people are liars. Final verdict. No appeal.

Ayah 14–31 · linked video: 02. An-Nur (Ayah 14-31)

Ayah 14

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِى مَآ أَفَضْتُمْ فِيهِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

The same subject of the slander against Aisha (RA) continues. Allah ﷻ says that had it not been for His favor upon the believers and His special mercy in matters of this world and the next, there would have been great punishment upon them because of what they let out. The phrase “in this world and the next” implies that Allah ﷻ helped the believers in the immediate situation and gave them a means to earn His forgiveness, especially those who fell into this controversy and scandal, so they could make Taubah and save their Hereafter.

The word أَفَضْتُمْ literally means “to pour out,” like when floodwaters pour out beyond their limit. In Hajj, when you look at the people from a distance, it looks like a spill, which is why Allah ﷻ uses the expression أَفِيضُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفَاضَ ٱلنَّاسُ (pour out from where all the people are pouring out). Here, it means they let the words spill out in every direction. You can imagine water in a glass going everywhere when spilled. They let these words go in every single direction, spreading the rumor without control.

Had it not been for Allah’s favor, the great punishment would have touched them for what they had allowed to spread so carelessly.

Ayah 15

إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَاهِكُم مَّا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ وَتَحْسَبُونَهُۥ هَيِّنًا وَهُوَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ describes the process by which this rumor spread: إِذْ تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ - when you were allowing it to reach or get into touch with your tongues. There’s an internal struggle when something is in the mind of a person. Should these thoughts meet my tongue or not? The word تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ captures this meeting between thought and speech.

Perhaps someone hesitated, thinking they shouldn’t say it, but then decided they needed something interesting to say, so maybe this could carry a conversation forward. They allowed their tongues to touch these words, to execute these words.

Then the ayah continues: وَتَقُولُونَ بِأَفْوَاهِكُم - and you were saying with your mouths what you have no knowledge of. Notice the progression. It starts with تَلَقَّوْنَهُۥ بِأَلْسِنَتِكُمْ (your tongues) and then moves to بِأَفْوَاهِكُم (your mouths). The tongue is a part of the mouth, so why mention both?

When someone just kind of says something, they move their tongue a little. The mention of the tongue captures that hesitant beginning. But when someone becomes aggressive and has a lot to say, has a mouthful of trash to talk, then the entire mouth is mentioned. Here there’s a progression. Maybe they started saying something hesitantly, not wanting to talk too much about it, or afraid to say too much. But then once the conversation snowballs and gets worse, the mouth starts running.

When you say someone “said something with their mouth,” that addition of “with your mouth” is actually an expression of anger. It’s like saying “shut your mouth” or “I’m going to yank that tongue out of your mouth.” These are expressions of displeasure. The One who gave you the tongue is expressing His anger: you’re using your tongue for that? You’re using your mouth for that?

We should keep track of how the mouth progresses through this surah. Previously in Surah An-Nur, the mouth was held open out of torture as punishment. Now it’s running loose with rumors.

مَّا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ - what you have no knowledge of, what you don’t possess any knowledge of. وَتَحْسَبُونَهُۥ هَيِّنًا - and you are assuming it to be very light, no big deal. The word هَيِّن describes something that’s easy, that goes down easy, that doesn’t take any effort whatsoever.

This is the same word the angel uses when telling Maryam (AS) about her miraculous child. She asks how she could have a child when no man has touched her, and Allah ﷻ responds: هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ - it is easy on Me, no big deal for Me. It doesn’t take any effort for Me to produce a child from you. I have that creative power.

Now people are using that same attitude toward spreading rumors: “Take it easy, I’m just saying, it’s not a big deal.” Allah ﷻ responds: وَهُوَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمٌ - but as far as Allah is concerned, it’s huge. It’s a really big deal.

Ayah 16

وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ سَمِعْتُمُوهُ قُلْتُم مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَـٰذَا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ هَـٰذَا بُهْتَـٰنٌ عَظِيمٌ

Why wasn’t it the case that when you first heard it, you should have said: مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَـٰذَا - it’s not appropriate for us, it’s not our place, it’s not becoming of us to be talking like this. We don’t possibly exist in the position to be speaking about this subject. It’s not becoming of us. It’s not our place. We are not that kind of creation. We will never be in the position to be speaking about this.

Then immediately: سُبْحَـٰنَكَ - how perfect You are! Notice they didn’t even say سُبْحَانَ ٱللَّهِ. They said سُبْحَـٰنَكَ (Subhanaka) - turning directly to Allah ﷻ. You’re too perfect for something like this to be said by a slave of Yours. It would be undignified. It would be beneath Your dignity that is owed to You.

The word سُبْحَانَ is used in the Quran when people engage in blasphemy, when they commit shirk, when they say things like Allah has taken a son. Allah ﷻ says: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ - He is above that, He’s too perfect for that. There is no shirk in this ayah, but the believer says سُبْحَـٰنَكَ because he realizes this rumor is blasphemy. This is something Allah ﷻ Himself doesn’t like. That’s why the previous ayah mentioned using your mouth like this.

هَـٰذَا بُهْتَـٰنٌ عَظِيمٌ - this is a huge, aggressive accusation. بُهْتَان is an accusation against somebody, but a very serious accusation, even beyond slander (ifk).

Ayah 17

يَعِظُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَن تَعُودُوا۟ لِمِثْلِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ is advising you: get your act together. Don’t let it happen again. أَن تَعُودُوا۟ لِمِثْلِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا - that you better not come back to do something like this ever again, anything like this, even remotely like this, ever.

The word مِثْلِهِۦٓ - “the likes of this” - is really important. Why? Because now Muslims reading the Quran might find the excuse to say, “Oh, but that was about that specific situation with Aisha (RA). It’s not about us.” Allah ﷻ says anything like this, the equivalent of this, anything remotely similar - don’t ever do it. إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ - if in fact you have iman, if in fact you are true believers.

Ayah 18

وَيُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ is clarifying His miraculous signs to you over and over again. وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - and Allah is fully knowledgeable, full of wisdom.

Ayah 19

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَن تَشِيعَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةُ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ - no doubt about it, the people, the kinds of people who love أَن تَشِيعَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةُ - that shamelessness should spread. The word تَشِيعَ means to become widespread, to become commonplace. شَاعَ means a population increased or something became popular.

Allah ﷻ says the people who love that shamelessness should spread, should become commonplace among the Muslims. In the context of the Sahabah (RA), they wanted conversations about shamelessness, the discourse about shamelessness, to increase among the Muslims. Even if they’re not doing something wrong themselves, they’re talking about it.

They have entire shows dedicated to what kind of dress someone was wearing at an awards ceremony. If Muslims are watching that stuff, there’s a group of people that love that these kinds of things should spread among the Muslims. They should be just like that. They should have no problem watching filth, talking about it. It should be easy for them.

لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - those kinds of people particularly have painful punishment in this world and in the next, and in the Hereafter. وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ - and in fact, He’s the One who knows. You have no idea.

Ayah 20

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

How many times in this surah has Allah ﷻ said “had it not been for the favor of Allah and His mercy”? Count them. This time, Allah ﷻ actually mentions the consequence, whereas previously He left the conclusion unstated.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ - and that Allah is extremely compassionate, sympathizing, sympathetic to your problems, always merciful.

Ayah 21

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَّبِعْ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - those of you who claim to believe. لَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ - don’t follow the footsteps of Shaytan. Spreading rumors is now equivalent to following the steps of Shaytan.

وَمَن يَتَّبِعْ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ - and whoever would follow the footsteps of Shaytan, فَإِنَّهُۥ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ - then know that he commands to الْفَحْشَآءِ - shamelessness, lewdness, vulgarity, indecency - and الْمُنكَرِ - evil deeds.

مُنكَر means evil in and of itself that nobody thinks is good, but مُنكَر also means that which is denied, that which is unheard of, that nobody’s ever seen before. People deny that this can even exist.

Let’s look at how this applies to society. We have a society in which, more and more globally, fahsha is becoming commonplace. An entire industry is behind it, and it has entered homes, schools, workplaces - every walk of life. When Shaytan’s command is being followed in fahsha, then his other commands are also followed. He gains authority over you.

Look at the progression mentioned elsewhere in the Quran, like in Surah Al-Baqarah. Whoever follows the footsteps of Shaytan - who’s in control at first? The follower, because he’s following. You could stop following if you want. But then Shaytan commands fahsha. When somebody commands, they’re in a position of authority. You started following him a little, and actually you handed him the authority. It’s like he put shackles on you, and now he makes you follow. You can’t even help yourself. He creates addictions in you.

In that kind of society, you’ll end up committing crimes that are unheard of, unspeakable crimes. Crime has always been there in human society - murder, robbery, crimes of passion. But when fahsha spreads, Allah ﷻ says munkar will spread, and he’ll command you to do things that are just weird.

Isn’t it the case that in postmodern society we’re seeing the kinds of crime, the nature of crime, that’s never been seen before? Kids walking into schools with guns. Mothers harming their own babies. Random mass shootings. This is unheard of behavior. But Allah ﷻ says this is actually a progression. The first thing to go is you start following the footsteps of Shaytan, and shamelessness goes away. When shamelessness goes away, your human decency goes away. And when your decency goes away, so does your humanity. Eventually, you’re not even a human being anymore.

Even animals don’t do what human beings are able to do. A predator will eat until it’s full and then stop. It’ll attack if it’s afraid or hungry, not because it’s in the mood. This is the kind of thing Shaytan can lead a society into.

وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ - keep counting how many times this phrase appears. This time Allah ﷻ actually mentions the consequence. The first time and every other time, He left the consequence unmentioned. He said “had it not been for the favor of Allah,” and then stopped. But now:

مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا - none of you would have been pure, ever. None of you would have attained a state of purity. زَكَاة is purity of wealth, purification of wealth. The verb زَكَىٰ means to become pure, but it also means to develop and grow. It means to sprout like useful plants, and it also means to get stronger.

Allah ﷻ says if it had not been for His favor upon you, then every trick of Shaytan would have made you weaker and weaker and weaker. You would never have become pure. Allah ﷻ would never have warded off or canceled the effects of Shaytan’s whispers from you. It is a favor of Allah that He continues to make you stronger and gives you opportunities.

زَكَىٰ here also means none of you would have been pure in the matter of attraction between men and women. Fahsha is so strong that none of you would have been able to resist it. It’s a favor of Allah ﷻ that He gave you the strength of iman that holds you back - the same بُرْهَان (clear proof) that was given to Yusuf (AS).

مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا - none of you would have ever been able to stay away. Without iman, guarding your chastity becomes impossible. Someone might say, “I’m saving myself, I’m going to wait,” but without iman, you’re going to cave in. Iman is supposed to be protection from these things.

What activity do we do every day that protects our iman? Salah. What does that say? إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ - “Indeed, salah prevents shamelessness and munkar.” Why did Allah ﷻ say that? Because without iman protected through salah, these temptations become overwhelming.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ - however, Allah cleanses, purifies, dignifies whoever He wants. If Allah ﷻ is going to purify somebody, then they will remain pure. All of us want that gift to happen.

That’s why the Quran says: فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ - don’t declare yourselves pure. Don’t think, “Oh, I’m above the temptation. I don’t have that problem. You have to give these people this advice, but these ayahs aren’t about us. They’re not about our MSA. They’re not about our masjid. Let’s give this advice to someone else. We’re strong Muslims, MashaAllah. We attend conferences. We don’t need this advice.”

Allah ﷻ says don’t declare yourself pure. هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ - He knows best who is righteous. Don’t judge: “Why are they talking about us like that? Why are they judging us?” I’m not judging anybody. Allah ﷻ already made the judgment.

وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

Ayah 22

وَلَا يَأْتَلِ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْفَضْلِ مِنكُمْ وَٱلسَّعَةِ أَن يُؤْتُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلْيَعْفُوا۟ وَلْيَصْفَحُوٓا۟ ۗ أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Now comes the case of Abu Bakr As-Siddiq (RA) - a man with a soft heart when it came to executing Allah’s law, yet his heart was extremely soft for the Messenger ﷺ. Between the two, the Messenger ﷺ wins. But on top of that, his heart was ultra soft for his daughter.

Every father in the Muslim world, when they read these ayahs, should think about their own daughter. What kind of pain would a father go through if he hears accusations made about his daughter? Unforgivable. The kind of rage and frustration that a father would feel about his daughter being talked about - I don’t know if that can even be compared with anything else. Especially a father with iman - because fathers without iman, fahsha is no big deal to them, so it’s no big deal for their daughters either. But a father with iman who raised his daughter with haqq, and then somebody accuses his daughter - the amount of rage and frustration is indescribable.

Abu Bakr’s (RA) relationship with Aisha (RA) isn’t just one of father and daughter. She’s his daughter and mother at the same time. She’s his daughter physically, but she’s also his mother because she is the mother of the believers. On the one hand, he has love for her because she’s his child, his pride. On the other hand, he has love for her because she is the pride of the Ummah. She is the beloved of his beloved ﷺ. It’s such a deep relationship.

One of his own Mistah ibn Uthathah - he was the son of Abu Bakr’s aunt, his first cousin, his maternal cousin - got caught up in this scandal like Hassan ibn Thabit (RA) and ended up saying some things. Mistah was bankrupt, and Abu Bakr (RA), who was wealthy, used to take care of him and give him an allowance.

Look at the size of this man’s heart. When the Prophet ﷺ describes Abu Bakr (RA), there’s a reason he says, “If I were to take a khalil (intimate friend) from mankind, I would take Abu Bakr, but he is my brother and companion.” Any one of us would think: I feed him, I give him clothes, I give him shelter, I pay him, and he said that about my daughter? He said that about the mother of the believers?

But Abu Bakr’s heart was so big. The manifestation of his anger was simply: I’m just not going to give him that allowance I used to give him. It wasn’t a punishment like harming him. It was just: I’m not going to do you favors anymore. That’s what it was. That’s where you see the size of the heart of this man. His great anger turned into, “Okay, I just won’t give him money anymore. That’s the decision I made.”

Allah ﷻ uses the plural here out of respect and regard and grandeur: وَلَا يَأْتَلِ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْفَضْلِ مِنكُمْ وَٱلسَّعَةِ - those who possess great favor among you and who possess huge expanse. سَعَة (sa’ah) is used in a general sense for someone who has wide capacity in something.

For example, in Surah Al-Baqarah, when Talut was chosen as king, Bani Israel complained: وَلَمْ يُؤْتَ سَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلْمَالِ - he hasn’t been given sa’ah (expanse) in terms of wealth. But here there’s no qualification mentioned. Allah ﷻ says: he possesses favor, and he’s got a lot of expanse - but expanse in what?

Allah ﷻ kept saying “had it not been for the favor of Allah” throughout these ayahs, and now He says: here’s one who’s been given favor. And he’s got السَّعَةِ - expanse. Allah ﷻ didn’t specify expanse in what. He didn’t say expanse in wealth. It’s general, meaning: he’s got wealth, faith, patience, love, loyalty.

Abu Bakr As-Siddiq (RA) is complemented in so many ways in the Quran. In Surah At-Tawbah, he was called ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ - the second of the two - which literally means he was the second of the two in the cave with the Prophet ﷺ. But he’s also the second of the two because he was the second person after the Prophet ﷺ - the first person to believe, so he became the second of the two alongside the Prophet ﷺ. It’s like when they’re in the cave, it’s “you and me again, just like at the start.”

There’s a famous hadith recorded by Tirmidhi: “We used to compete in good deeds even in the times of Jahiliyyah. Then he preceded me in becoming a Prophet ﷺ, so I followed him.” The Prophet ﷺ said, “If he had beaten me to this, I would have followed him.” SubhanAllah, what a statement.

This person - Allah ﷻ says: وَلَا يَأْتَلِ - these people shouldn’t take an oath. يَأْتَلِ means to take an oath not to do something. They shouldn’t take an oath not to give أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ - those who are close in relation, وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ - those who are poor, وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - and those who have migrated in the path of Allah ﷻ.

These are three groups, but actually they’re three descriptions of the same person - Mistah. First, he’s a close relative. Second, he’s very poor. Third, and most beautifully, even though he made this mistake, Allah ﷻ compliments him: وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - he did actually make Hijrah. He’s from the Muhajirun.

Allah ﷻ didn’t say “He’s one of those who sinned” - if He had said that, his good deeds would have been canceled. Instead, Allah ﷻ mentioned his ultimate good deed, which is the great title that every Sahabi enjoys. He is a Muhajir fi sabilillah. The first statement - he’s close in relation - is not a compliment, it’s just who he is. Being poor is not a compliment. But المُهَـٰجِرِينَ - Allah ﷻ mentioned that as a compliment, giving us between the lines a reason to think: Allah went easy on him, so I should go easy on him too.

But then Allah ﷻ offers this: وَلْيَعْفُوا۟ - and he should forgive. The word عَفَا means to forgive someone such that you cannot punish them. Allah ﷻ says he shouldn’t punish him. But was Abu Bakr (RA) punishing him? Actually, he was just not doing favors for him. But Allah ﷻ is saying: your standards are so high that for you, not doing a favor is a punishment.

For anybody else with lesser standards, this wouldn’t count as punishment. But for Abu Bakr (RA), Allah ﷻ says: you should just let it go. There should be no vengeance. There should be no retribution. He should just forgive.

But just because you don’t punish physically or you start giving him food again doesn’t guarantee that you don’t remember what happened, or that other aspects of your relationship aren’t scarred. Is it possible that you forgive somebody but your relationship with them just isn’t the same anymore? It’s possible. You forgave, but you moved on - but you didn’t totally move on.

Allah ﷻ adds: وَلْيَصْفَحُوٓا۟ - and he should turn the page. When you turn the page, what can’t you see anymore? The page before. He shouldn’t just forgive - he should turn the page. Let it go. Let his heart completely heal. Don’t even remember what he did. It’s like the page has already been turned. That chapter is over.

It’s so hard to do that. Many of you have people in your personal life, especially close relatives. And here we’re learning a general lesson: close relatives are in a really good position to hurt your feelings. Uncles, cousins, brothers, sisters, parents, spouse - they’re in a really good position to hurt you because they’re so close to your heart, which means they can stab it easily too.

So it’s hard to forgive, and then to forgive and forget - that’s literally what this is - is really hard to do. Allah ﷻ offers an incentive. For a person of iman who says “I can’t let go, I can’t let go,” if you have iman, then this incentive should be enough. If this incentive isn’t enough, then the only problem left is a problem of iman.

What is this incentive? أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ - don’t you love, wouldn’t you all love, that Allah would cover your mistakes?

Think about this equation of forgiveness. I’m so angry at him, so angry at her, so angry at them. Put a weight on that anger. It’s 15,000 tons of anger. Then put a weight on how badly do I want Allah ﷻ to forgive me? You decide which weighs more. Because Allah ﷻ is offering you: you really should forgive, you really should turn the page, because if you do, I am guaranteeing you forgiveness. Wouldn’t you like that? Or is your anger more important? You decide.

Abu Bakr (RA), when this was revealed, said: “Yes, I want that. I’ll let it go.” To let go of something like this is normally impossible for a human being. My daughter was accused. My Messenger ﷺ was tortured psychologically by someone I thought was loyal to me. I can expect betrayal from an enemy, but my own nephew? On top of that, the mother of the believers? How could he do this? I can’t understand why he would do such a thing.

But Abu Bakr (RA) said: “No, let it go. Turn the page.” He would hug Mistah like he hugged him before. He loved him like he loved him before, because of this forgiveness. He said: “Yes, I’ll take it.”

وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - Allah is extremely forgiving, always merciful.

You and I are tested with our iman through our relatives. Are we going to take this deal from Allah ﷻ or not?

There are people about to go to Hajj who say, “I’m about to go to Hajj. I don’t know. I can’t forgive. There are some people - I don’t know what to do. There’s a big dark hole in my heart and there’s no way I can forgive some people. I can’t get myself to ask for forgiveness from them either. I can’t do it. I just can’t.”

You can tell yourself anything you want, but the truth doesn’t submit to you. You and I submit to the truth. I can say “I can’t, I can’t, I can’t,” but Allah ﷻ says: لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا - Allah does not burden anyone with something they can’t bear.

So when you say you can’t, and Allah ﷻ says you can, I have to go with Allah ﷻ. Stop telling yourself you can’t. Snap out of it. There’s no “I can’t.” There’s nothing holding you back. Shaytan wants to keep the fires of rage burning. Allah ﷻ brings the extinguisher and says: I’ll cover your mistakes if you take this deal.

Ayah 23

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ ٱلْغَـٰفِلَـٰتِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ لُعِنُوا۟ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

That was Abu Bakr’s situation (RA). Allah ﷻ didn’t say his name but used general speech because it applies to his situation and will apply forever after.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَرْمُونَ ٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتِ - those who throw this kind of vile accusation against dignified, protected, fortified women. ٱلْغَـٰفِلَـٰتِ - who aren’t even aware, who don’t even know. ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ - true believing women.

لُعِنُوا۟ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - they are to be cursed in this world and the next. وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ - and they have huge punishment, great punishment.

Ayah 24

يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ - the Day on which their own tongues are going to be testifying against them.

We are learning that we are a dictator over our body. A dictator makes people do things they don’t want to do. My tongue is running right now and I’m making it do it. I’m commanding my tongue, my hands, my eyes to go where they go.

On the Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ will take that dictatorial authority I have over my body away. Every one of the members of this population of mine - this body - is going to rebel and testify against this dictator.

The tongue will say: “He made me say this.” The feet will say: “He made me go there.” The hands will say: “He made me do this.” The eyes will say: “He made me look at that. I didn’t want to. I was created by Allah ﷻ. I was created in submission, but he made me do it. He forced me to look.”

The eyes will testify. In this world, the thing that is always loyal to you under any circumstances is your own body. If nothing else, your body is loyal to you. On the Day of Judgment, your body is not even loyal to you. Your body says: “No, you used me for wrong. Allah ﷻ made me, and everything Allah ﷻ made, He made in submission. So I was rebelling. I was forced to rebel against Allah ﷻ because of you. You are my enemy.”

Your body becomes your own enemy.

وَأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم - and their hands are going to testify, their feet are going to testify. بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - because of what they used to do, of what they used to do.

Ayah 25

يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ دِينَهُمُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ٱلْمُبِينُ

يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ دِينَهُمُ ٱلْحَقَّ - the Day on which Allah will compensate them their payback, their retribution, truthfully. The true compensation for what they did. The true retribution for what they did.

دِين here means retribution, the true judgment, the true verdict for what they did.

وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ٱلْمُبِينُ - and they will know that on that Day, for sure, they’ll get to know how true Allah is. That Allah ﷻ, in fact, is the clear and ultimately clear truth.

Ayah 26

ٱلْخَبِيثَـٰتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَٱلْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَـٰتِ ۖ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَٱلطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مُبَرَّءُونَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ ۖ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ

ٱلْخَبِيثَـٰتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَٱلْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثَـٰتِ - indecent, filthy, vile women are made for vile men, and vile men are there for vile women.

وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَٱلطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ - and good women deserve good men, and good men deserve good women. Good and pure men are for good and pure women. That’s how things are supposed to be. This is the natural order of things. That’s how it’s meant to be.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مُبَرَّءُونَ مِمَّا يَقُولُونَ - they are absolved from what these people say. Good and pure people are absolved. They are completely innocent. They are disassociated from all the rumors and accusations that are made against them.

لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ - they are going to have forgiveness and a gracious, noble compensation, noble provisions.

I should mention something about كَرِيمٌ here. Their dignity is being questioned in these accusations, so Allah ﷻ says the compensation they’ll get is full of dignity - رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ. Dignity is important in that context.

Ayah 27

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ وَتُسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ - Muslims, listen up. Let me tell you something about the decency of visiting each other’s homes.

You want to go to somebody’s house. You wanted to talk to them about something unannounced. You showed up. Don’t enter the homes. Even if you see the door open, don’t do it.

لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ بُيُوتِكُمْ - don’t enter homes other than your own homes.

I’ll add to that as an added precaution: when I go home and see cars parked outside that I know aren’t mine, I know my wife has friends over. So for those few minutes, for me personally, that’s not my house right now. I’ll just say, “Hey, can I come in?” or “Is everything clear? I just want to go upstairs. Can I go?” Don’t just walk in even though you have the keys.

Don’t quote the ayah like “there’s no problem.” Maybe there are women at your house having a gathering. All the women are casually sitting there, and you walk right in. What have you done? Understand the principle. The idea is to announce yourself before you enter upon people.

Women who are casual, just sitting there, hanging out, chilling - sometimes at sisters-only gatherings they can get dressed up, put makeup on, do things they don’t normally do. You shouldn’t just walk in. You wouldn’t want anybody to walk in on your wife, on your sister. These are somebody’s wives, somebody’s sisters. Have some respect.

This is so trivial in our homes, it’s become so casual, and it’s disgusting.

I’ll tell you a story. I used to work at a masjid and lived in a house adjacent to the masjid. I wasn’t the imam - I’m never going to be, I’m not qualified - but I was the education director. I ran the Sunday school. Some guy, you know, when you’re at the masjid everybody thinks you’re the imam. So some guy had a question about Islamic finance or something I know I’m not going to answer. He figured I’m the imam. I was sitting there eating lunch. He opened the door and walked into my house. “Brother, I have a question.”

I jumped out of my chair and pushed him out of my house. I slammed the door behind me. “What do you think you’re doing?” “No, I just had a question.” “I’m sorry, you can’t walk into people’s homes. You can’t do that. If my wife was there, if my mother was there…” You know what that tells you? We don’t know Surah An-Nur. We don’t know. This is serious stuff. Don’t mess around with this.

Don’t just walk in. Sometimes we’re living in an age of ignorance, and the people whose home you go to, maybe they’re ignorant of Surah An-Nur. So when you come, they say, “Just come in, come in.” You should say, “Hey, is it clear?” Just to be safe. If they don’t know, help them out. “Is it clear?” “Okay, come in.”

حَتَّىٰ تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ - until you make yourself known. تَسْتَأْنِسُوا۟ is a beautiful word because it’s about making yourself known through a sound or action. You could say “Are you there?” or even not talk to them directly - just say something so the people inside hear you. People in the home are sensitive. They know if somebody’s outside. The kids go running: “Somebody’s there!” You make yourself known.

You don’t have to proclaim yourself and walk in. That’s why doorbells are there now, and knocks are there.

Now, you showed up early. You showed up at 8:00 when they thought you’d come at 9:30. You showed up at 7:00 because you missed them so much or you wanted to hang out. You obviously don’t have real social common sense. You showed up an hour and a half before, when nobody wants to see you. They’re preparing, they’re hungry, they’re grouchy, they don’t want to deal with you at all.

وَتُسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا - and say salaam to the entire family.

You show up early, and maybe they say, “Could you come later?” Some people say, “Seriously? I came to your house and you’re telling me to leave? I’m never going back there again. These people hurt my feelings.” That’s going to be dealt with too in the next ayah. That’s actually your fault. Don’t go earlier, and if you go earlier and they say go away, then go away. Say, “Yeah sure, I’ll leave.”

ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ - that is better for you so that you may make an effort to remember.

You know why this is coming in this surah? This surah was firstly about الزَّانِيَةُ وَٱلزَّانِى - adultery, fornication, etc. You don’t want any man to just walk in and look at the women of the household or for them to look at him. Who else is roaming around in the house? Shaytan: “She’s kind of cute.” “Who’s that handsome guy? He’s my brother’s friend, but he’s cute.” That’s how it begins. It can lead to other things. It does. That’s how it happens. It’s a casual look first. Allah ﷻ knows about the stealing of the eyes.

Ayah 28

فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فِيهَآ أَحَدًا فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِن قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۖ هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ

فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فِيهَآ أَحَدًا - and if you don’t find anybody answering the door, you don’t find anyone in them, فَلَا تَدْخُلُوهَا حَتَّىٰ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ - then don’t enter them until permission has been granted to you.

وَإِن قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ - and if it is said to you, “Could you please leave?” then go back. It doesn’t even say “kindly leave.” It just says “leave.” They don’t even have to be nice about it. I’m not saying you should be mean if you’re the host - no. But the guest is being told: Listen, the host has rights too. The host’s home should not be entered until it’s appropriate for you to enter. If it’s not a good time, ٱرْجِعُوا۟ - go back, فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ - then return.

And don’t start using your imagination: “I wonder what they’re up to in there.” No, no, no. Allah ﷻ says: وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا۟ - don’t spy around. إِنَّ بَعْضَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ - some assumptions are sin. Don’t make assumptions. Don’t use your imagination. “Okay, I’ll leave.” That’s it.

هُوَ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ - that is purer for you. وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ - and Allah ﷻ is fully knowledgeable of what you do.

Ayah 29

لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ فِيهَا مَتَـٰعٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ

بُيُوت are places where you spend the night. Linguistically, بَات is where you spend the night, and it could be workplaces too if you’re working late night - the office becomes a bayt because بَات literally means to spend the night.

People hearing this might think, “Oh my God, I can’t even go into the warehouse, the farm, the barn, any place where I have any work to do at night, any construction site. I should just knock on the door and wait for permission? I’m not going to walk into the office? I’m going to stand outside the Walmart and wait for permission? How is this going to work? How does the city deal with this?”

Allah ﷻ says: لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ - then it is no harm upon you, there’s no harm upon you to enter بُيُوتًا غَيْرَ مَسْكُونَةٍ - commercial properties, properties that aren’t lived in. In other words, the classroom, the office, the store, the airport. The hotel lobby - you can go in. However, the hotel room - knock, because that becomes a bayt.

فِيهَا مَتَـٰعٌ لَّكُمْ - in which there is utility for you, there’s a means of work, you’ve got to go there for some purpose. Those kinds of homes, go ahead.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ - and Allah knows the things you expose and the things that you hide.

In other words, Allah ﷻ knows when you try to use this to say, “Well, that room nobody really lives in, so I kind of went in,” or “They’re usually upstairs” - when you try to twist and turn Allah’s words. Allah ﷻ knows what you show and what you hide. Allah ﷻ also knows when you use those kinds of facilities for developing illicit relationships, when there are office romances. Allah ﷻ knows about that too.

Just because Allah ﷻ says you can enter those homes without permission, the purpose for which this is given is protection after protection after protection from zina, from inappropriate interaction that starts with innocent romantic conversations and then evolves into something else. If you’re up to those things at the office or the warehouse or at Walmart or whatever else, Allah ﷻ knows about that.

Ayah 30

قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَغُضُّوا۟ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ وَيَحْفَظُوا۟ فُرُوجَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَزْكَىٰ لَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ

قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - tell the believers يَغُضُّوا۟ - they should lower. The verb غَضَّ is used for two things: voices and eyes, gaze.

يَغُضُّوا۟ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ - lower their eyes. وَيَحْفَظُوا۟ فُرُوجَهُمْ - and as a result, they should guard their privates.

Allah ﷻ says there’s a really deep connection between the eyes and zina. You see, you like, you pursue. That’s how it happens. So lower your eyes.

By the way, مِنْ - قُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - believing men and women, because the masculine in Arabic is inclusive of both genders.

ذَٰلِكَ أَزْكَىٰ لَهُمْ - that is the pure way for them, closer to purity for them.

What are we learning? When you don’t watch your eyes, your heart is getting dirtier and dirtier and dirtier. This is so beautiful and relevant because the passage we’re about to enter is going to be about a clear glass that’s not dirty, that’s pure, where the oil is pure. But these are things that make that heart, that glass, dirty. Every inappropriate gaze is a stain on that heart, and another stain, and another stain, until the whole thing is covered in darkness.

When the heart is covered in darkness, your one problem is shamelessness. But then all the other evils come out. That’s why فَإِنَّهُۥ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ - fahsha leads to munkar.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ - what a beautiful ayah. Allah is fully aware, fully knowledgeable, has full news of the things you manufacture. It’s not just يَعْمَلُونَ - what you do - but يَصْنَعُونَ - manufactured, in the sense of manufacturing.

What is involved in manufacturing? Planning. You don’t just manufacture a car - you build blueprints. There are parts and pieces and pieces. Allah ﷻ knows which one of you has game, has a game plan. First you’re going to look at her, then you’re going to wink, then she’s going to think you have a bug in your eye, and you’re going to go over and say, “Are you on Facebook? I just wanted to let you know because I want to invite you to future Islamic events. Please take my Facebook.”

Then it goes further. The guy’s got a whole plan laid out. He’s going to start an innocent conversation, ask for homework: “Do you have yesterday’s assignment? I was wondering if you have it.” There are 300 students in the class. Why did you go to her? Why did you go to her to ask?

You’re manufacturing an entire strategy. “Today I’ll talk to her once. Tomorrow, twice. Then I might say, ‘Hey, I’m going to go get some pizza. You want some?’ Then next time I’ll say, ‘Hey, you want to come along? Want to go for a walk? Aren’t you in that other class?’ - that I just signed up for because I knew you signed up for it.”

He’s got a whole plan, and she says, “Oh, what a coincidence! SubhanAllah, I had no idea!” She’s oblivious.

Some girls are sharp, but then there are some girls whose innocence is annoying. They have no idea what’s going on. It’s ridiculous. “He was just asking me for my homework.” What planet do you live on? “This guy in college, he’s asking a lot of questions about Islam. Is it okay if I talk to him at Starbucks? We talked for four hours yesterday about Sharia, and today he wants to know about Aqeedah. He’s got so many questions. Should I talk to him?” No. “But he says he’s more comfortable talking to me.” “Next week he was talking to me about Islam and he said if he converts, would I marry him? I don’t mind, but do you think that’s okay?”

I get those questions all the time. The guy manufactures an entire plan. It’s not just one thing - he’s building a factory literally. Plans go into this.

Girls should not be naive to that. They should learn to keep a stern voice. A stern voice scares the wits out of a guy. “Hey, so are you on Facebook?” “Who wants to know?” It’s over. He’s not going to mess with you again. It’s over. Done. You won’t have that problem again.

All women have to do is have a stern voice. The scariest thing to a man is the stern voice of his mother, his sister, his wife. “When are you coming home?” - as opposed to “When are you coming home?” There’s a world of difference.

Keep your voice sharp. You have to be tough. Girls naturally - I have four daughters, I know - girls are just giggly, smiley. They don’t even have a reason to smile. My girls and their friends get together and they don’t even talk. They just look at each other and laugh.

This is important. Why? Because when a guy sees a girl smile, what happens to him? He gets a picture in the back of his head: “Oh yeah. She doesn’t even realize what she’s doing. She has no idea, no clue, the effect she has. Her smile is magical.” He looks drugged. She doesn’t even know.

Now he’s going to plan and scheme. “I love to make her laugh,” she says. “I talked to him because he’s funny.” إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ - Allah knows all of this stuff.

If you lowered your eyes, you wouldn’t have been hypnotized by her smile. If you weren’t hypnotized by her smile, you wouldn’t start concocting the entire plan, changing your course schedule, signing up for all the classes where she is, somehow becoming her lab partner. You wouldn’t have done all of that. You would have saved yourself and another believer from a lot of Shaytan, from a lot of sin.

But this is what happens. And SubhanAllah, we have the blessing of Facebook where we can put all kinds of pictures - religious boys and girls, group photos at the MSA - so you could just stare at her smile now for hours. You can do that.

“You’re too extreme. Take it easy.” I’d rather stay extreme. I’m sorry, I have daughters to worry about. I think they’ll survive without Facebook.

I joined Facebook because I wanted to get in touch with my cousins from Pakistan. I left Pakistan in 1993 and haven’t gone back. I joined Facebook and got hundreds, then thousands of friend requests. Even the phrase “friend request” bothered me. “I don’t know this girl. What are you saying? You want to be my friend?” I don’t even like the language.

So I just stopped. Even if I befriended a brother, it would take me to his page and there are all these other pictures. Why? Why are you doing this? Why do you have a picture of you hugging your wife? That’s not right. There’s something wrong with that. This is supposed to be behind closed doors. Why do you think Allah ﷻ said to knock first? Why are you putting it on public display now? You don’t even have to knock - just click and you’ve entered people’s homes, the very thing Allah ﷻ didn’t want us to do. We said, “Okay, we’ll maintain the knocking part, but we’ll just take everything that’s supposed to be private and put it on the web for the world to see.” It’s absurd.

I’m not a mufti. I can’t tell you what’s halal and haram. I’m not going to pass a fatwa that Facebook is haram or Twitter is haram. I’m saying use it responsibly. These devices have brought a lot of good to the world. A lot of transparency has been brought to journalism. Revolutions, popular uprisings that couldn’t have been imagined came about through Twitter and Facebook. It’s good stuff, but use it responsibly. These teachings are not a joke.
